article_title: Community structure and the behavior of transnational sustainability governors: Toward a multi-relational approach

It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). As a result, we had to regroup and instead encourage coders to identify each one according to the two categories, and then to note cases where they simply could not figure out how to best apply the code book (which meant we needed to revise and provide better guidance) and/or be clearer about whether a paper was in or out of our universe. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: Community structure and the behavior of transnational sustainability governors: Toward a multi-relational approach Luc Fransen Department of Political Science, University of Amsterdam, Amsterdam, The Netherlands Jelmer Schalk Institute of Public Administration, Leiden University Campus The Hague, The Hague, The Netherlands Graeme Auld School of Public Policy and Administration, Carleton University, Ottawa, Ontario, Canada Abstract Hundreds of transnational private governance organizations (TPGOs) have emerged in recent decades to govern social and environmental conditions of production using voluntary standards. A debate persists over whether the ties among different TPGOs and other organizations create a professional community that affects the behavior of TPGOs. To help resolve this debate, we analyze multiple ties among agriculture TPGOs to offer a more robust exploration of community structures and their potential effects for three forms of TPGO behavior – coordination, collaboration, and isomorphism. Our aggregate mea- sure of ties reveals a thin community dominated by older TPGOs and TPGOs advancing a broad notion of sustainability that were created by Solidaridad, the World Wildlife Fund, and/or Unilever. The clearest community structures are built from ties that exhibit the potential for not actual collaboration, coordination, and isomorphism. Thus, while there exists convergence toward an emergent TPGO-community, obstacles remain to more intense behavioral effects for TPGOs. Keywords: agriculture, policy community, private governance, social network analysis, sustainable development. 1. Introduction Transnational private governance organizations (TPGOs) set standards, procedures, and governance models for various sustainability issues and global industries. They have become a prominent global governance mechanism to address environmental and social problems arising from business-as-usual practices in global supply chains. TPGOs like Utz and Fairtrade are increasing their impact on agricultural practices globally with growing numbers of certiﬁed farms, businesses endorsing their standards, and certiﬁed-product sales (Potts et al. 2014). TPGOs, however, do not operate in isolation from each other. They interact with other TPGOs that are focused on either the same or different problems (Eberlein et al. 2014). Varied consequences ﬂow from these interactions. Interactions may limit the undesirable effects of competition among TPGOs in the same sector or issue area (Fransen 2015). They may help TPGOs address problems that a single TPGO cannot address alone, such as land-use change and inconsistencies or duplications in auditing practices (Auld 2014a). They may act as transmission belts for policy diffusion and organizational convergence among TPGOs (Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009). As such, these interactions affect whether TPGOs solve the problems the actors that support them are keen to resolve. Interactions therefore represent a key characteristic of an emerging community of TPGOs – a self- aware grouping of individuals and organizations linked by shared practices and a sense of identity – that has the potential to greatly inﬂuence the aggregate impacts of TPGOs on global problems. Existing research recognizes the importance of any such emerging TPGO-community for collective action fea- turing coordination and collaboration among organizations (Bartley & Smith 2010) and for the garnering of Correspondence: Luc Fransen, Department of Political Science, University of Amsterdam, Nieuwe Achtergracht 166, 1018 WV Amsterdam, The Netherlands. Recent empirical work has sought to describe the structure of TPGO communities in different issues areas. By structure, we mean here the emergent characteristics of a group of organizations and individuals that affect the relationships that group members have with each other and their individual and collective behaviors. Studies measure different types of ties among actors, and ﬁnd communities of slightly different shapes, sizes, and densities. The ties examined include interme- diaries and interlocks (Bartley & Smith 2010), mutual references and membership in partnership organizations, (Green 2013), and mutual employment (Fransen et al. 2016). These studies have two shortcomings. First, they often measure one type of tie to identify TPGO community structure. Yet different ties may identify different communities, which means this approach undermines efforts to robustly detect community structure among policymakers. Second, current studies on TPGOs, and beyond (cf. Heemskerk & Takes 2016), do not thoroughly conceptualize and justify how a given type of tie adequately measures the kind of social, political, or economic consequences they expect to ﬂow from a latent community. As a result, different studies may mean different things when they refer to communities. To resolve these two shortcomings, we investigate two research questions. We ﬁrst ask a descriptive question: how are TPGOs interlinked? Describing the varied ties that link TPGOs then allows us to ask: What kinds of communities do these links create, and what consequences might these different communities have for TPGO behavior? Our empirical case is the agriculture sector where we examine 16 TPGOs that advance overlapping attention to sustainability. Following recent literature and policy vocabulary (Bitzer et al. 2012; Marx & Wouters 2014), we understand sustainability-focused TPGOs to promote economic, and/or social, and/or environmental criteria for production. Agriculture is a useful sector for study because of the signiﬁcant sustainable development challenges faced. Moreover, the overall density of agriculture TPGOs facilitates an exploration of different community struc- tures and their potential consequences for TPGO behavior. Finally, in the agricultural sector there is a long pedi- gree of signiﬁcant TPGO coordination challenges. This makes agriculture a useful sector in which to examine claims about the likelihood that TPGO communities increase in strength and undergird TPGO coordination and convergence. We measure three types of ties among TPGOs: membership and linkages among TPGOs in meta-governance programs that aim to be intermediary organizations managing TPGO communities (cf. Adler 2008, p. 204; Pro- van & Kenis 2008); ongoing partnerships among TPGOs; and uptake and recognition of policy ideas and tools in the policy documents of various TPGOs. We combine these measures with the community structure identiﬁed by Fransen et al. (2016) through examination of the professional and educational backgrounds of all current staff and governors of TPGOs governing sustainability issues over a 10-year period. Conceptually, we distinguish ties as a potential for versus ties that directly evidence three forms of TPGO behavior – coordination, collaboration, and convergence toward similar policies (isomorphism). These distinctions help clarify whether communities con- form to two conceptions: a thin community where political discord and varied organizational perspectives can persist, or a thick community with little political discord and common organizational perspectives (Djelic & Quack 2010). This multi-relational empirical approach has several beneﬁts. It descriptively does justice to the real-world complexity of links among organizations and professionals and the varied communities these ties may undergird. More signiﬁcantly, it also allows us to explore more reliably whether the policy community structure identiﬁed makes it more or less likely that TPGOs at present behave in particular ways or in the future are likely to behave in particular ways. Are ties more likely to resolve coordination problems among competing TPGOs? Are ties likely to help address challenges resulting from the limited problem scope of TPGOs? Or might ties increase the likelihood of isomorphism toward a uniform perspective of sustainability? That is, the varied TPGO community structures identiﬁed through different ties have potential implications for how well TPGOs alone, and in coopera- tion with each other, address the problems that the actors initiating them hoped that they would. Our analysis proceeds in nine parts. First, we review existing literature to justify the importance of our multi-relational approach. Second, we clarify our conception of community and our empirical strategy. Third, we deduce propositions for empirical explorations. Fourth, we explain methodological choices and the data gathered. Fifth, we describe our data and common characteristics of relationships. Sixth, we develop our empir- ical strategy for community detection. Seventh, we analyze the multi-relational TPGO network using social © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 L. Fransen et al. Community structure in TPGOs Page 3: network analytical terminology. Eighth, we discuss the implications of our empirical results. A ﬁnal section concludes. 2. Communities in transnational private sustainability governance Why should we care about the structure of TPGO communities? Two answers are apparent. A ﬁrst is that com- munity structure affects the nature and effectiveness of collective action aimed at addressing mutually identiﬁed problems. In this respect, different community structures serve as an independent variable explaining variation in collective action. Early literature implicitly examined this premise by assessing how pre-existing communities affected collective action that generated TPGOs. Consider two contrasting formation processes. A ﬁrst wave of agriculture TPGOs arose via local and diffusely structured communities that linked individuals and organizations exchanging ideas about the problems of industrialized and intensive forms of agriculture (Dankers & Liu 2003; Guthman 2004). In 1972, these national organizations working on organic agriculture launched the International Federation of Organic Agriculture Movements (IFOAM) in order to exchange infor- mation on organic practices and develop common principles (Langman 1992). Fairtrade certiﬁcation is similarly rooted in a diffuse community based on the work of alternative trade organizations established on a small scale in Europe and North America since the 1950s. By 1997, the Fairtrade Labeling Organizations International (now Fairtrade International or FLO) was established and now coordinates the standards and labeling work of over a dozen national initiatives (Raynolds 2000). A second TPGO formation process has involved a different community structure – one dominated by a few actors that decide to supply a TPGO alone or with a small group of partners. The World Wildlife Fund (WWF) has been prominent in this role; it has helped establish initiatives for commodities such as palm oil (Schouten & Glasbergen 2011), soy (Elgert 2012), and most recently, sugar and cotton (Sneyd 2014). Particular retailers, gov- ernment development agencies, and American philanthropic foundations have also helped launch different TPGOs (Ponte 2014; Sneyd 2014). Thus, in these two contrasting processes, communities of differing structures facilitated collective efforts to form standards, and later certiﬁcation procedures, for organic, fair trade, and other sustainability-targeted forms of agriculture. A second reason community structure matters is its role as a source of legitimacy for a TPGO. The evaluative judgments of the individuals and organizations comprising a TPGO community, it is argued (Bernstein & Cash- ore 2007; Black 2008), are the basis for the organization’s legitimacy. In this respect, it is not just that actors turn to TPGOs as a solution to problems, but that once a TPGO exists, the organization will turn to external actors and community norms as a way to garner material and symbolic support such that the TPGO can claim legiti- macy as a private rulemaker (Boström 2006). Different community structures can thus be expected to support varied norms and offer distinct material and symbolic resources to TPGOs. Features of the communities that pro- vide TPGO support, such as levels of cohesion (Bartley 2003), the breadth of the community’s policy concerns (Auld 2014b), or the different views of the problem and appropriate solutions held by its constituent members (Haedicke 2016), are hypothesized to affect programmatic operations such as rule implementation and effective- ness (Heilmayr & Lambin 2016). The growing numbers of TPGOs means they are increasingly at the center of community structures, relative to other policymaking entities and actors holding a stake in sustainable agriculture. Studies are now examining whether community structures inﬂuence the interactive effects of TPGOs on sustainable production, rather than studying TPGO effects on production in isolation from one another (Eberlein et al. 2014). Thus, unlike the litera- ture that examined the features of pre-existing communities or communities external to TPGOs as, respectively, the basis for collective action or legitimacy, it is increasingly necessary to understand how ties among TPGOs cre- ate communities that may themselves facilitate collective action or shape organizational legitimacy. Consider ﬁrst collective action. The proliferation of TPGOs complicates collective action. Competition among TPGOs that focus on similar policy issues and industries may stimulate revisions in standard setting and gover- nance relevant for effective and legitimate policymaking and governance (Overdevest 2010). Competition may also lead to implementation challenges, rising costs, and confusion among stakeholders (cf. Marx & Wouters 2014; Fransen 2015). In addition, when TPGOs in the same industries have different policy foci they may be less © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Community structure in TPGOs L. Fransen et al. Page 4: productive at addressing the sustainability issues that surpass their respective problem scopes (Auld 2014a). Embedding TPGOs in communities can therefore potentially facilitate collaborative responses to these challenges (Fransen et al. 2016). With legitimacy, external communities may assist TPGOs to establish and maintain legitimacy from key con- stituencies. For this reason, community structures can engender common norms of what is considered appropri- ate TPGO governance across TPGOs (Dingwerth & Pattberg 2009; Bernstein 2011). In contemporary TPGO policymaking, therefore, describing the structure of communities may help us assess the possibility of collective action to address particular problems and the nature of TPGO legitimation dynamics. Yet two challenges limit our ability to understand how community structures affect these two outcomes. First, current work on TPGO interactions and community measures different types of ties. Second, how these measures conceptually relate to different TPGO behaviors and behavioral goals is rarely illustrated. Underlying these limita- tions, we argue, is an absence of careful attention to how communities are conceptualized and operationalized. We begin to resolve this fundamental shortcoming in the following section. 3. Conceptualizing and measuring transnational private governance organization (TPGO) communities In an inﬂuential volume, Djelic and Quack (2010, pp. 3–21) argue that as the act of governing transcends nation- states, it is increasingly likely that communities of professionals spanning borders may shape transnational gover- nance. Such professionals may include persons belonging to public as well as private organizations that are work- ing for for-proﬁt or non-proﬁt goals, as experts, interest representatives, or bureaucrats. According to Djelic and Quack, these professionals can be considered members of a community once the following necessary attributes are in place: …mutual orientation of members, articulated around a common identity and/or a common project; a sense of reciprocal dependence; a form of active engagement and involvement from at least a minority of members; […] translating into a sustained sense of belonging. (2010, p. 10) Following Weber et al., they argue that communities have a relational and a symbolic component, and can be thinner or thicker where the necessary attributes they identify play a role to greater or lesser degrees. Community building is aided by but does not require direct contact between people and organizations. A shared sense of des- tiny can emerge from information ﬂows that are not accompanied by direct exchange between people. Accord- ingly, similarity and homogeneity may be more or less prevalent among the actors constituting a community (Djelic & Quack 2010, p. 12). In DiMaggio and Powell’s (1983) terms, isomorphism may occur in terms of increasing similarity in policies and perspectives on policies among community members. In emphasizing variation in the thickness of communities, Djelic and Quack mirror understandings of com- munity in other political science ﬁelds, including studies of corporatism and national public policy (Jordan 1990; Smith 1991) or epistemic communities in international relations (Haas 1992). In the former literature, communi- ties are also distinguished according to the degree to which these are more or less cohesive in ideological or polit- ical orientation, the degree to which these are able to spur common political projects, produce isomorphic policy effects among their members, or override the interests of individual community members relative to the commu- nity interest. In thinner communities, political discord remains and consensus may effectively be minimal (Smith 1991). In thicker communities, isomorphism prevails (Rhodes 1988; Blom-Hansen 1997). In a chapter for the Djelic and Quack volume, Bartley and Smith (2010) analyze ties among TPGOs and adopt the term transnational community of practice. Following Adler (2005), a community for them is a “set of actors involved in a joint enterprise that is constantly being renegotiated by its members and held together through relationships of mutual engagement” (Bartley & Smith 2010, p. 351). Bartley and Smith hold that within this community “cooperation and growing coherence exist alongside conﬂict and debate” (2010, p. 348). Yet they note that “[e]ven representatives of competing certiﬁcation initiatives have engaged in loose forms of coopera- tion” (Bartley & Smith 2010, p. 348). As such, Bartley and Smith use a thinner version of community than the convergent community type referred to by Dingwerth and Pattberg or Bernstein and Cashore.1 Empirically, Bartley and Smith measure ties as cross- © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 L. Fransen et al. Community structure in TPGOs Page 5: references in policy documents on websites and individuals in 2001 and 2006. They claim that initially, certifying TPGOs emerge out of smaller, relatively disparate communities with varying political commitments. However, increasing linkages among these previously disparate communities through professional interactions offers a structure for the emergence of a transnational community of practice. Other recent work addresses TPGO community structures, sometimes invoking the community concept explicitly, sometimes not. Certain studies reinforce Bartley and Smith’s ﬁndings (Green 2013; Henriksen 2014; Henriksen & Seabrooke 2015), but others propose a slightly different diagnosis. Bitzer et al. (2012), for instance, ﬁnd limited ties among agriculture TPGOs measured as common engagement in cross-sectoral partnerships to promote sustainable cocoa. According to these authors, this absence of ties signiﬁes competitive concerns. Simi- larly, Fransen et al. (2016) attempt to detect communities in the ties of agriculture TPGOs, analyzing links through current and past employment of all known professionals (board and non-board) working for TPGOs. They ﬁnd scarcely any community: neither across TPGOs focused on different sectors, TPGOs focused on similar sectors or sustainability issues, nor among TPGOs with similar sustainability problem deﬁnitions or similar evo- lutionary histories. Within these studies, connections are usually measured with one type of tie, which may be analyzed at differ- ent levels (i.e., individual vs. organization). This is problematic as community structures may be tie speciﬁc and each tie might be idiosyncratic in terms of the organizational goals, resources, and behavioral context concerned. Consequently, as experts on social network analysis observe, reliance on one type of tie raises the risk that any inference drawn will be insufﬁciently grounded in the context of social and political action of interest to the researcher (Kilduff & Brass 2010). We therefore argue that the structure of TPGO communities should be assessed via multiple types of ties. To move in this direction, we draw theoretically on Djelic and Quack’s useful distinction among relational and sym- bolic dimensions of community structure in transnational governance, and on their idea that communities may be thicker or thinner. We seek to ﬁrst distinguish among socio-relational ties, in terms of their ability to constitute thicker or thin- ner communities. In our view, if we want to capture ties constituting thicker communities, we need to look at actual ongoing collaboration and coordination among TPGOs. This means substantive policy exchange and a publicly stated commitment to resolve issues together. In this way TPGOs show collective action at work. Turning to the potential for collaboration, coordination, and isomorphism among TPGOs, we treat these as thinner kinds of links among community members, because these do not yet signify substantive agreement or alignment. They only establish connections that imply exchange of a varied and not speciﬁed nature among the organizations and their employees. As literature on professional networks and communities argues, this can be done in two ways. A ﬁrst approach examines intermediary organizations that seek to bring TPGOs closer together and have them exchange ideas, build trust, and encourage collaborative projects and isomorphism (cf. Adler 2008; Provan & Kenis 2008). We therefore examine TPGO immersion in so-called meta-governance organiza- tions like the Global Social Compliance Programme, the Dutch Sustainable Trade Initiative (IDH) or the Ethical Tea Partnership (Loconto & Fouilleux 2014; Fransen 2015). In these initiatives, TPGOs are invited to coordinate; their participation implies the aspiration to look for ways to cross-fertilize. We also include organizations afﬁli- ated with the International Social and Environmental Accreditation and Labeling (ISEAL) Alliance. The vast majority of these organizations have subscriber or associate membership status with ISEAL. This means they have a similar aspirational relation to collaboration and isomorphism as a result of mutual adjustment among TPGOs as the participants in aforementioned initiatives. Participation in these organizations does not necessarily serve as direct evidence of coordination, collaboration, or isomorphism among TPGOs, because this is not a requirement for participation. A second approach treats individuals as intermediaries among organizations. Thus, we examine links among the employees and board members of TPGOs through current employment or board interlocks and through past employment (cf. Heemskerk & Takes 2016). We also conceive of this as a socio-relational tie that should capture the potential to exchange ideas and build trust among TPGO professionals. These personal ties could be stepping-stones to potential policy coordination, collaboration, and isomorphic effects. They could even be strate- gically viewed as such, particularly when it comes to board-level ties. But there is no certainty that these ties will lead to substantive policy interactions. © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Community structure in TPGOs L. Fransen et al. Page 6: Turning to Djelic and Quack’s idea of symbolic ties – ties that denote isomorphism – we propose that the most comprehensive and systematic way to gauge these ties is to investigate references in standard poli- cies of TPGOs to other TPGOs or to similar third party sources or organizations. We expect these ties to capture actual isomorphic effects, as they portray similarity in describing what sustainability means, and/or similarity in which organizations receive praise as informing a particular perspective on sustainability policy. We are agnostic as to whether we should interpret these links as thin or thick ties. On the one hand, a link constituted through a similar reference to a United Nations (UN) document may mean that both TPGOs sub- scribe to a very generic policy idea or hope that people think of a TPGO’s work as being related to the UN.2 On the other hand, one could argue that even the name checking of the same third-party, such as an intergovern- mental organization treaty text or a scientiﬁc institute’s deﬁnition, is signiﬁcant. Even in this minimal gesture, TPGOs have a substantive similarity, linking an idea to a similar epistemic and/or political source, and agreeing on a similar source for authority. In any event, irrespective of the precise character of this tie, we include it in our analysis because of our interest in variation among different ties that have been previously investigated by researchers to describe TPGO policymaking communities. Table 1 summarizes these indicators of community structure, their characteristics, and their theoretical impli- cations for TPGO behavior. We expect that different types of ties will capture different processes of community formation among TPGOs; consequently, they should have different implications for the policies and practices of TPGOs. Some are relevant for isomorphic effects, some for collaboration and coordination among TPGOs; others are relevant for all categories of TPGO interactive effects. As discussed, TPGO community structures can be interpreted descriptively for their consequences for both legitimation dynamics and for TPGO collective action potential. We focus on the latter, because reporting on both collective action and legitimacy would prove too cumbersome and because we believe coordination prob- lems have more acute implications for TPGO policymaking. However, our analysis will focus on one category of overlap between collective action-focused and legitimacy-focused studies of communities: increased similarity of policies as a result of mutual references, which signiﬁes both collaborative potential and legitimation among TPGOs. Future studies should focus on other aspects of legitimation dynamics. 4. Expectations of TPGO community structure Building on the distinctions set out in the previous section, we develop descriptive propositions that specify which community structures we expect to ﬁnd in the empirical analysis. By structure, we mean the character and extent of ties that exist among the individuals and organizations identiﬁed in our empirical analysis. We know from social network analysis that both the detectability and the composition of community structure may depend on the ties investigated. Communities are more detectable to the extent that groups of actors have higher connectiv- ity within their group and lower connectivity across groups. On the other hand, composition captures the TPGO and orga- nizational membership of speciﬁc communities within each type of relationship. Generally, we expect variation in detectability and composition to be particularly pronounced when we have theoretical reasons to believe different types of ties will mean different things for the behavior of TPGOs. On this basis, we propose: Hypothesis 1: The detectability and composition of communities are tie speciﬁc. Turning to the speciﬁc nature of TPGO communities, the literature provides different explanations for inter- actions among TPGOs, as driven by rational-economic calculations (Prakash & Potoski 2007), political tensions (Bartley 2007), and/or sociological-ideational forces (Bernstein & Cashore 2007). TPGOs are therefore seen respectively as either self-interested organizations, embodiments of a political compromise among the interest groups driving these organizations, or institutionalizations of a norm. An integrative strand in the literature claims that all of these factors matter to some degree in understanding TPGOs (Bartley & Smith 2010; Reinecke et al. 2012; Turcotte et al. 2014). In this view, TPGOs are torn between ideological and material motives and cooperative and competitive impulses when engaging peer organizations. Moreover, TPGO communities exhibit both collaborative and competitive characteristics among organizations, and include ideological afﬁnities and con- troversies among policymakers and professionals. These different views lead to different expectations about the long-run structure of communities and their inﬂuence on TPGO behavior. However, in the shorter run, we hypothesize, following Reinecke et al. (2012), that community structure is more likely to comprise thinner ties among TPGOs that do not automatically imply sig- niﬁcant policy exchange and adjustments. We expect to observe these ties because TPGOs will want to protect their organizational survival and their core ideological commitments. Thus in looking at different socio-relational ties among TPGOs we expect: Hypothesis 2: For socio-relational ties, communities are more detectable for thinner types of TPGO ties than thicker ones. We present rival propositions on the relevance of symbolic ties following our agnostic perspective on the character of these ties (as explained in the previous section). In one scenario these ties should be sparser. Here, the isomorphic behavior of TPGOs – like socio-relational thick ties – involves careful weighing of organizational interests and the ideological positions of the TPGO relative to an external environment of (competing) TPGOs and other organizations (cf. Reinecke et al. 2012). In another scenario, by contrast, these ties are expected to be more numerous because TPGOs name check other organizations with relative ease (cf. Bartley & Smith 2010). We thus expect: Hypothesis 3a: Symbolic ties are sparser than socio-relational thin ties. or Hypothesis 3b: Symbolic ties are denser than socio-relational thin ties. We know of no theoretical reason to expect a priori why one of the above two expectations should trump the other. We therefore treat the issue as an empirical question. These propositions focus our attention on the degree to which TPGO-community structure may make TPGOs adopt coordinative, collaborative, and/or isomorphic behaviors. Where few ties, or mostly thin ties, are identiﬁed, there is at present little sign of and little potential in the future for collaboration to occur, compared to a situation where a lot of ties and also many thick ties are found. 5. Research design, data, and deﬁnitions TPGOs exist in multiple sectors. Estimates indicate there might be several hundred in operation around the world (International Trade Centre 2016). We focus on TPGOs active in agriculture because, ﬁrst, agriculture activities are crucial for the socio-economic and environmental challenges addressed by contemporary governmental and non-governmental policymakers. Second, it is a sector with many different TPGOs that are assumed to compete in the market and/or struggle over the appropriate deﬁnition and implementation of sustainability policies © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 7 Community structure in TPGOs L. Fransen et al. Page 8: (Reinecke et al. 2012; Auld 2014a). It is therefore a useful ﬁeld to study in light of our ambition to develop a more precise understanding of what ties among TPGOs, and possibly community structure, may look like. Third, private governance in agriculture has a relatively long history, and clear challenges to do with coordination and convergence exist that TPGOs should address. This means that it is a sector that has characteristics consistent with projective theories that predict a TPGO policy community should arise there, with members of such a com- munity addressing coordination challenges (Bernstein & Cashore 2007). Our analysis therefore may reﬁne the empirical identiﬁcation of any such transnational communities and their consequence for future coordinative, collaborative, and isomorphic behaviors of TPGOs. In sum, understanding TPGO community structure in agriculture is of political and theoretical importance. Although we cannot assume that our ﬁndings for this sector are representative of TPGOs as a whole, our results should serve as the basis for hypothesizing about their implications for TPGOs focusing on other sectors. Agriculture is broadly understood to include ﬂowers and cotton next to food and energy crops. We do how- ever limit our analysis to land-based production, and we exclude ﬁsheries and aquaculture. To prevent this deﬁni- tion driving results, we examine community structures for agriculture as a whole and for subsets of standards that may plausibly constitute smaller communities because of a similar crop or issue focus. National standard organizations that are afﬁliated with these TPGOs surface in our analysis if TPGOs have ties with them. We select transnational private governance organizations for our sample because our interest is in the structure of transnational policy communities. Sustainability standards are understood to cover economic, social, and environmental criteria for agricultural production (Bitzer et al. 2012; Marx & Wouters 2014). This is consistent with the Brundtland Commission’s broad deﬁnition of sustainable development covering economic, social, and/or environmental components. Our interest is in communities of and around policymakers. By policymakers, we mean individuals that for- mulate, decide among, or implement the policies of a TPGO (Fransen et al. 2016). We deﬁne community struc- ture as the emergent characteristics of a group of organizations and individuals that affect the relationships that group members have with each other and their individual and collective behaviors. We adopt the method used by Fransen et al. (2016) as a baseline for determining the TPGO population. They focused on TPGOs linked by common employees, using data from online resumes between 2002 and 2012 (i.e. the employment ties we describe in Table 1). Their cases are a more appropriate baseline than Bartley and Smith’s (2010) study of certiﬁcation programs, as these latter authors excluded TPGOs that do not use certiﬁcates even if they set voluntary sustainability standards. Our approach leads to a more exhaustive case selection than other studies that include only a subset of agriculture TPGOs (Bitzer et al. 2012; Henriksen 2014; Henriksen & Seabrooke 2015).
